Pr
Praseodymium
Rare Earth Element

Praseodymium

Discover the fascinating world of element 59, a silvery-white rare earth metal from the lanthanide series. Named from the Greek words for "green twin" due to its green salts.

Atomic Number
59
Atomic Mass
140.91 u
Period
6
Group
Lanthanide

Discovery

Discovered in 1885 by Carl Auer von Welsbach in Vienna, Austria. He separated praseodymium and neodymium from a material known as didymium, using fractional crystallization.

Scroll to explore

Atomic Structure

Explore the 59 electrons orbiting around the nucleus containing 59 protons and 82 neutrons. Click on particles to learn more.

Controls

Show electron shells

Electron Configuration

1s2
2s 2p8
3s 3p 3d18
4s 4p 4d 4f21
5s 5p8
6s2
[Xe] 4f³ 6s²

Particle Legend

Atomic Data

Atomic Radius
247 pm
Covalent Radius
203 pm
Van der Waals
239 pm
Electronegativity
1.13

Chemical Properties

Understanding the chemical behavior of Praseodymium through its electron configuration, oxidation states, and unique properties.

Oxidation States

+3, +4

Most stable as +3, can form +4 in some compounds. The +3 state is characteristic of lanthanides.

Reactivity

Moderate

Reacts slowly with oxygen at room temperature, rapidly when heated. Forms Pr₂O₃ oxide layer.

Magnetic Properties

Paramagnetic

Exhibits paramagnetic behavior due to unpaired 4f electrons. Used in permanent magnets.

Melting Point

931°C

Relatively low melting point for a rare earth metal. Boiling point is 3,520°C.

Orbital Visualization

Click on an orbital to visualize its shape. The 4f orbitals are key to Praseodymium's unique properties.

Electronic Configuration

Highlighted: Valence electrons (4f³ 6s²)

Physical Properties

Density6.77 g/cm³
Crystal StructureDHCP
AppearanceSilvery-white
State at 20°CSolid

Important Compounds

Explore the key compounds of Praseodymium with interactive 3D molecular structures. Rotate and zoom to inspect atomic arrangements.

Praseodymium Oxide

Pr₂O₃ / Pr₆O₁₁

A black or greenish-black powder that forms when praseodymium reacts with oxygen. The most common commercial form is Pr₆O₁₁.

Properties

  • Black to greenish-black color
  • Melting point: 2,183°C
  • Density: 6.88 g/cm³
  • Insoluble in water

Applications

  • Ceramic pigments
  • Glass coloring
  • Catalyst applications
  • Solid oxide fuel cells
Characteristic color

Compound Comparison

PropertyPr₂O₃ PrCl₃PrF₃
StateSolid (powder)CrystallineSolid
Water SolubilityInsolubleHighLow
Primary UsePigmentsSynthesisOptics

Real-World Applications

From powerful magnets to aerospace alloys, Praseodymium plays a crucial role in modern technology and industry.

Permanent Magnets

Praseodymium is combined with neodymium to create powerful Pr-Nd magnets used in various high-tech applications.

Key Applications

  • Up to 5% Pr in NdFeB magnets
  • Improves corrosion resistance
  • Enhances high-temperature performance
  • Used in hard disk drives, speakers, and headphones
$500M+
Annual Market
15%
Yearly Growth
Praseodymium demand is expected to increase significantly due to electric vehicle adoption and renewable energy expansion.

Summary & Future

Praseodymium stands as a cornerstone of modern technology, with its importance only set to grow in the coming decades.

Essential Element

One of 17 rare earth elements critical to modern technology

Growing Demand

Increasing importance in clean energy and electric vehicles

Global Impact

Used in technologies that shape our daily lives

Key Takeaway

From its green-tinted salts to its role in powering electric vehicles, Praseodymium exemplifies how rare earth elements are fundamental to sustainable technology and innovation.

Future Research Directions

1Development of more efficient Pr-based permanent magnets
2Advanced optical materials for quantum computing
3Sustainable extraction and recycling methods
4New alloys for next-generation aerospace
5Enhanced energy storage applications
